question: Which high-risk imaging or clinical features are used to select patients with uncomplicated TBAD for early TEVAR?
source_access_type: Original publisher PDF held in private local source workspace
source_version_or_date: European Journal of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery. 2014;48(3):285-291; appended 2015 author-list erratum present
relevant_sections: Current risk stratification of uncomplicated type B patients
Problems using thrombosis of the false lumen as an endpoint

study_characteristics.population: 61 patients with acute uncomplicated type B aortic dissection randomized within 14 days of symptom onset
study_characteristics.intervention: Best medical treatment plus Gore TAG
study_characteristics.follow_up: CT at 3 months and 1 year; reported clinical and imaging follow-up through 1 year
study_characteristics.analysis_note: The primary endpoint used intention-to-treat analysis. The study was designed around a remodeling/rupture composite and was not powered for mortality.

primary_fields.pmid: Matched to registered PMID 24962744; PMID is not printed in the supplied publisher PDF
primary_fields.journal: Matched: European Journal of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery

source_identity_validation.metadata_warning: The supplied PDF appends a 2015 erratum correcting one ADSORB Trialist name and removing duplicated trialist names. It does not correct study methods, results, or conclusions and does not change the identity of the 2014 article.

extracted_claims.claim_text: ADSORB enrolled acute uncomplicated TBAD within 14 days of symptom onset and randomized patients to best medical treatment alone or best medical treatment plus early endovascular repair.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Between December 2008 and December 2010, patients presenting with an acute uncomplicated type B dissection and without any evidence of connective tissue disease were randomised within 14 days of onset of symptoms. All patients were immediately treated with blood pressure control and were randomised to best medical treatment (BMT) alone or BMT with endoluminal repair using a Gore TAG device (TAG+BMT). The TAG treatment had to be done within 48 hours of randomisation.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Eligibility and treatment timing, not a validated prognostic rule or evidence that a baseline feature modifies TEVAR benefit.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The trial's uncomplicated population excluded rupture, impending rupture, and malperfusion.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: The AD history had to be less than 14 days, and exclusion criteria were rupture, impending rupture, malperfusion.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The abstract provides the explicit exclusion list. This separates the randomized uncomplicated cohort from complicated TBAD; the article does not present these exclusions as candidate high-risk predictors.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: ADSORB included double-lumen dissection but excluded other acute aortic syndrome variants and required anatomy suitable for a proximal endograft landing zone.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Patients with a double lumen dissection were included, but not other variants of acute aortic syndrome. ... The proximal landing zone had to be 2 cm in length and the device at least 15 cm long.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The ellipsis omits the intervening DeBakey classification definition on the same page. These are study/device eligibility constraints, not prognostic predictors.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: More than half of logged potential candidates were not enrolled, mainly because of anatomical limitations, and the exact candidate denominator was unavailable.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: No log book was consequently filled out, so that we cannot give any exact number of potential candidates for randomisation. More than half of the patients logged were not included for various, mainly anatomical, limitations.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: This limits generalizability and prevents a complete accounting of screening exclusions; it does not identify which anatomical features predicted clinical outcome or treatment benefit.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: For trial classification, DeBakey IIIB was assigned when dissection involved the abdominal aorta or the superior mesenteric, inferior mesenteric, or any renal artery; otherwise it was IIIA.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: The proportion of type IIIB dissections using DeBakey's classification criteria was determined if the dissection involved the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), any renal artery or the abdominal aorta. Otherwise it was a type IIIA dissection.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: This is a baseline anatomical classification rule. Branch-vessel involvement here does not itself mean malperfusion and was not validated by ADSORB as a predictor of outcome or differential TEVAR benefit.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Most randomized patients had DeBakey IIIB disease, but ADSORB reported this as a baseline distribution rather than a prognostic or treatment-selection result.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: In the TAG+BMT group 77% were type IIIB (n.s.) and in the BMT group 84%.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Descriptive randomized-group distribution. The nonsignificant baseline comparison does not establish equivalence and the study does not show IIIB status predicts TEVAR benefit.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Randomization and stent-graft placement occurred early in the acute phase, with median treatment timing approximately five days after symptom onset.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: The median time from onset of symptoms to randomisation was 4.8 (range 0.3-14.3) days for the BMT group and 4.6 (range 0.3-13.3) days for the TAG+BMT group. The placement of the TAG device was a median 5.5 (range 1-14) days after onset of symptoms (0.9 days after randomisation). All patients were therefore treated within 14 days of the onset of symptoms.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Acute-phase timing descriptor; ADSORB does not compare acute with subacute timing or validate an optimal timing window.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Baseline maximum false-lumen and true-lumen diameters did not differ statistically between randomized groups; these measurements were baseline descriptors, not prognostic thresholds.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: There was no baseline difference in average maximum false lumen diameter (Fig. 2) (TAG+BMT 23.9 vs. BMT 22.1 mm, p = .791) or in maximum true lumen diameter (Fig. 3) (TAG+BMT 22.5 vs. BMT 23.6 mm, p = .83) between the two groups.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Randomized-group baseline measurements. These values are not cutoff points, validated prognostic predictors, or treatment-effect modifiers; nonsignificance is not interpreted as equivalence.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The ADSORB discussion lists partial false-lumen thrombosis, aortic diameter greater than 4 cm, false-lumen diameter greater than 22 mm, large proximal entry tears, and entry tears on the concave inner curvature as previously reported poor-outcome factors.
extracted_claims.evidence_category: Narrative summary of external risk literature
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Factors of importance for poor outcome include partial thrombosis of the false lumen, aortic diameter greater than 4 cm, false lumen diameter of more than 22 mm, large proximal entry tears, and entry tears on the concave inner curvature of the distal arch as opposed to the convex outer curvature.
extracted_claims.section: Current risk stratification of uncomplicated type B patients
extracted_claims.source_location_note: This is a narrative synthesis citing references 13 and 27-32, not an ADSORB eligibility rule, prespecified subgroup analysis, or trial-validated predictor of TEVAR benefit. Thresholds are not numerical clinical outcomes.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; interpretation and cited-source applicability require specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The article describes conflicting evidence about false-lumen thrombosis as a risk marker and warns that CT-based thrombosis assessment is method-dependent.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Complete thrombosis of the false lumen is associated with better survival than a fully patent false lumen in non-stent-grafted patients, but partial thrombosis has the worst outcome. Another study, though, shows that thrombosis of the false lumen was not an independent factor for intervention.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Narrative summary of references 14 and 15, not a randomized ADSORB finding. Printed p. 290/PDF p. 6 additionally states that CT diagnosis of false-lumen thrombus is inaccurate compared with blood-pool-agent MRI.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; applicability requires specialist review of the cited evidence

extracted_claims.claim_text: ADSORB demonstrated differential one-year aortic remodeling after randomized treatment, but the authors did not establish that a baseline anatomical feature predicted benefit or that remodeling proved improved survival.
extracted_claims.evidence_category: Randomized remodeling finding with interpretation limit
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: The question remains as to whether endovascular treatment with a stent graft in the acute phase of acute type B aortic dissection is associated with improved survival compared with medical treatment alone, and can only be demonstrated with a larger randomised controlled trial and longer follow-up.
extracted_claims.section: Problems using thrombosis of the false lumen as an endpoint
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The trial's remodeling results appear on printed p. 288/PDF p. 4. No prespecified feature-by-treatment interaction or prognostic-model analysis is reported; remodeling must not be treated as survival evidence or as validation of a selection threshold.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: ADSORB's patient-selection and outcome interpretation are limited by expert-center referral selection, incomplete imaging, and lack of power for mortality.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Most of the recruiting centres were tertiary referral hospitals, so there may be a bias in the selection of patients with type B aortic dissection included in the trial. The good results achieved from endovascular repair may not be replicated by less experienced centres. There were challenges in obtaining quality follow-up imaging resulting in 14 failures due to imaging issues/dropouts. Additionally, the ADSORB trial was not powered for mortality.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: These limitations restrict generalizability and clinical-outcome inference, and reinforce that ADSORB did not validate a high-risk patient-selection model.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; pending specialist review

limitations: ADSORB randomized treatment, not high-risk-feature-defined treatment selection; it reports no validated prognostic model or feature-by-treatment interaction.
The listed poor-outcome features are a narrative summary of external studies rather than risk factors derived or validated in ADSORB.
Eligibility and baseline anatomical measurements must not be converted into prognostic thresholds.
One-year remodeling endpoints do not establish survival benefit.
The trial was small, was not powered for mortality, had substantial imaging failures/dropouts, and enrolled at experienced mainly tertiary referral centers.
ADSORB evaluated acute treatment within 14 days and did not compare acute with subacute or chronic timing.
contradictions: The article notes that false-lumen thrombosis has been associated with outcome in some external studies but was not an independent factor for intervention in another study.

The article cites an IRAD analysis associating initial aortic diameter below 4.0 cm with increased expansion, while its broader narrative also lists aortic diameter above 4 cm among poor-outcome factors; populations, endpoints, and methods may differ and require specialist interpretation.
reviewer_notes: Q03 extraction is restricted to selection-related eligibility, baseline descriptors, narrative risk-stratification statements, and explicit limitations. No ADSORB treatment outcome is represented as proof that a specific high-risk feature predicts TEVAR benefit. No numerical outcome records were created because the directly relevant numbers are eligibility thresholds or baseline descriptors rather than clinical outcomes.
